replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Michel Leduc phosphors are difficult to make at home mate as the properties are very dependant on process conditions - you can do it but they will probably not be very stable

author: @applesworld
text:
I don't see how the grainy finish would be a problem if a mid-coat copper plate finish was applied then polished prior to a nickel or chrome plate finish on top as the final coat.
It appears from my limited knowledge so far that the copper coating layer does a good job of 'filling-out' and smoothing over any surface imperfections.
I'm currently ( ha ha ), looking to buy a home DIY electroplating kit, so I think I ought to buy some of your conductive ink to see what results I can get on ABS.
I wish there where more people like you out there !

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you'd be surprised what people moan about mate lol - you don't often meet the sensible down to earth approach that you clearly have

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text: An electroluminescent ink/paint would be cool. I remember looking into ZnS before, and I remember the phosphorescent ability had something to do with the actual sizes of the zinc sulfide (monoclinic) crystals.  Another common compound is SrAl2O4, and is now more commonly used now for glow-in-the-dark materials having a brighter, more even luminescence than phosphorescent ZnS.  Doping the SrAl2O4 phosphor with tiny amounts of Eu and Dy rare earths, for example, will create a glow-in-the-dark phosphor that can last 50 hours once illuminated with UV light.  Very interesting!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is and i am working on it - the problem is EL phosphors are stupidly expensive - so I am busily making my own lol

text:

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya EL powders don't use phosphorus mate - they are mostly doped zinc sulfate and that's what i will use phosphor is just a hang on name for them

replies.author: @mikehebblethwaite3693
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I would like to offer a new line of thought if I may. Chemists and physicists don't normally mix as people but this seems to need both, bio too, Maybe you could introduce the ink as a plant food to say a Rhubarb leaf (massive) making it bond to the leaf as it does photosynthesis. Then leaf decay and leaves your sheet behind? just an idea as I have made copper sulphate solution go into a leaf and yes it kills it I just wanted a copper leaf skeleton for my solar hydro hobby (and it looks amazing).

replies.author: @mikehebblethwaite3693
replies.text: Mike Hebblethwaite P.S. my ink is Wood Glue and the rod out of a battery ground down into dust mixed in 50/50 gives 60 ohms yours is much better so I am using your method.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
